summary:
Beginning in the late Neolithic period, roughly 3500–3000 BCE, a number of Chinese settlements were enclosed by earthen or stone walls, representing some of the earliest examples of planned fortification in East Asia. Sites such as Chengziya in Shandong, Shimao in Shaanxi, and various Longshan-affiliated sites were enclosed by tamped-earth (hangtu) walls that could reach several meters in height and width. These walls reflect increasing social complexity, inter-community conflict, and the emergence of local political elites.

The construction of these walled towns required substantial organized labor and resource mobilization, suggesting the presence of governing authorities capable of directing collective efforts. Excavations at walled Neolithic sites have produced evidence of craft specialization, surplus food storage, and elite burials with prestige goods including jade, lacquerware, and painted ceramics. The tradition of walled settlements became a defining feature of Chinese civilization and persisted through the Bronze Age and beyond, ultimately culminating in the great city walls of historical China.
